publications.editedComment: The museum is a nested filtering puzzle with an explicit answer state. The collection can be searched, filtered by folder, status, review coverage, and discovery, sorted six ways, and shown as a grid or list. Selecting a Component swaps the broad catalog for one detail surface, while clearing selection restores the catalog. The detail combines `wonderlab-preview-host`, canonical metadata, editable curator fields, `component-review-feed`, and `reaction-card`; admin-only controls expose reconciliation and persistence. Store-derived counts summarize the four major status groups. Empty searches get a deliberate no-results panel. The manager does not dynamically execute arbitrary source—it passes the selected canonical identity to the bounded preview host and keeps the catalog state around it.

A respectable little blizzard, rendered upon a fixed full-window canvas. On mount, the canvas adopts the viewport dimensions and creates precisely 140 flakes with randomized position, radius, speed, horizontal drift, phase, and opacity. Every animation frame clears the surface, advances each flake downward with a sinusoidal sway, wraps horizontal escapees to the opposite edge, and returns flakes crossing the bottom to the top at a new x-coordinate. Pale circular fills provide the snow. The resize listener keeps the drawing buffer aligned with the window; unmount cancels the stored animation frame and removes that listener. Pointer events are disabled. The effect decorates the room, sir—it does not demand the room's attention.

The snow begins only after the canvas and its 2D context are available. A resized viewport becomes the field for 140 flakes, each carrying its own radius, falling speed, drift, phase, and alpha. `tick` clears the old sky, adds steady descent and a small sine-wave sway, wraps flakes at the left and right edges, and relights any flake that falls below the screen at the top. The next frame is stored so unmount can cancel it, and the same cleanup removes the window resize listener. The canvas is fixed above the page but ignores pointer input. Quiet construction: one lamp of animation, no store state, no user controls, and no snow left running after the Component leaves.

Oh! A tiny lightning purse—sorry, that's accurate. On mount it asks the Mana store to fetch the current balance, then the button shows either that number or infinity for a family plan. Opening the popover reveals the username, balance, cap, progress, refill-ready state or countdown, plus routes to the wallet and subscriptions. Family accounts swap the progress display for the unlimited-house-token message. A ready refill makes the closed button pulse. Clicking outside the widget or pressing Escape closes it, and both document listeners are removed on unmount. The links also close the panel before navigating. Nothing here spends or refills mana; it reports the store's state and points toward the places that can change it.

This is a compact resource readout with a properly contained popover. The Mana store fetches on mount; the button renders balance or infinity, and pulses only when a non-family refill is ready. Inside, ordinary accounts get balance-versus-cap progress and either a countdown or readiness label, while family accounts get the explicit unlimited-house-token message. Wallet and subscription links close the panel as they navigate. Document-level click and Escape handlers dismiss the popover, and unmount removes both listeners. The root containment check prevents inside clicks from closing it accidentally. Important boundary, darling: the widget displays and routes. It does not purchase, refill, reserve, or spend a single unit of mana. MONGO would still click it twice.

The panel loads the active User into a local form and keeps it synchronized when the store's user changes. Fifteen declared fields cover identity, biography, location, contact details, birthday, languages, timezone, and social URLs; textarea, date, email, URL, and text controls are selected from the field definitions. Saving builds a partial User payload with the current id and a parsed birthday, then delegates persistence to `userStore.updateUserInfo`. Success and thrown errors become visible status messages, with saving state disabling both submit positions. The header reports whether an ArtImage avatar id is connected, but avatar editing occurs elsewhere. With no user, the form is replaced by a guest-state panel. Broad manifest, single accountable save route.

A profile form made from a field spell rather than fifteen copied incantations. `profileFields` defines each editable key, label, input type, and placeholder; the template loops that list and gives biography the full row. A watcher immediately copies the current User into the reactive form, converting a valid birthday to `YYYY-MM-DD`. Save assembles the same keys into a partial User payload, includes the existing id, converts the birthday back to a Date or null, and calls the User store. The panel catches errors, announces success, and always releases its saving flag. Avatar linkage is displayed but not changed. No active profile means no form. Mischief approved: one source of field truth, two submit buttons, zero duplicate save logic.

This signpost has one destination and one closed gate. The full-size button displays the Kind paintbrush icon and calls `goToThemePage`. That function routes to `/themes` only when the Smartbar store says editing is off; editing mode deliberately leaves the user where they are. The Component also computes the current theme name from the Theme store and exposes it as `navLabel` for a parent that wants a label. It does not change themes, open a picker, or write Smartbar state. Even the hover scale is purely presentation. At this crossroads the rule is plain: browsing may follow the paintbrush, editing keeps its hands on the work already open.

The paintbrush button performs one disciplined navigation check. `isEditing` is computed from the Smartbar store; clicking routes to `/themes` only when that flag is false. Thus the icon cannot pull the user away while the Smartbar is in its editing state. Separately, `navLabel` reflects `themeStore.currentTheme` and is exposed to the parent, although the local template renders only the icon. No theme mutation, selection menu, confirmation, or local state exists here. The hover scale announces interactivity, but the store decides whether the action is permitted. Small controls benefit from firm boundaries: this one names the current theme for others and opens the theme page when the surrounding workspace is not occupied.
